2.5 Some Rules of Probability

Thm 2.3 If A , A c A , A c A,A^(c)A, A^{c}A,Ac are complementary events in a sample space, then
P ( A c ) = 1 − P ( A ) P A c = 1 − P ( A ) P(A^(c))=1-P(A)P\left(A^{c}\right)=1-P(A)P(Ac)=1−P(A)
Pf. S = A ∪ A c S = A ∪ A c S=A uuA^(c)S=A \cup A^{c}S=A∪Ac and this union is disjoint.
P ( S ) = P ( A ∪ A c ) = P ( A ) + P ( A c ) P ( S ) = P A ∪ A c = P ( A ) + P A c P(S)=P(A uuA^(c))=P(A)+P(A^(c))P(S)=P\left(A \cup A^{c}\right)=P(A)+P\left(A^{c}\right)P(S)=P(A∪Ac)=P(A)+P(Ac)
by Postulate 3. Also,
1 = P ( A ) + P ( A c ) 1 = P ( A ) + P A c 1=P(A)+P(A^(c))1=P(A)+P\left(A^{c}\right)1=P(A)+P(Ac)
by Postulate 2. Hence
1 − P ( A ) = P ( A c ) 1 − P ( A ) = P A c 1-P(A)=P(A^(c))1-P(A)=P\left(A^{c}\right)1−P(A)=P(Ac)
so P ( A c ) = 1 − P ( A ) P A c = 1 − P ( A ) P(A^(c))=1-P(A)P\left(A^{c}\right)=1-P(A)P(Ac)=1−P(A).
Cor 2.4 P ( ∅ ) = 0 2.4 P ( ∅ ) = 0 2.4 P(O/)=02.4 P(\varnothing)=02.4P(∅)=0 for any sample space S S SSS.
Pf. Let A = S A = S A=SA=SA=S and apply Thm 2.3.
Nb. The book uses A ′ A ′ A^(')A^{\prime}A′ to denote the complement of an event, but A c A c A^(c)A^{c}Ac is the more standard notation.
Thm 2.5 If A , B A , B A,BA, BA,B are events in a sample space and A ⊆ B A ⊆ B A sube BA \subseteq BA⊆B, then
P ( A ) ≤ P ( B ) P ( A ) ≤ P ( B ) P(A) <= P(B)P(A) \leq P(B)P(A)≤P(B)
Pf. Since A ⊆ B A ⊆ B A sube BA \subseteq BA⊆B, we can write
B = A ∪ ( B ∖ A ) = A ∪ ( B ∩ A c ) B = A ∪ ( B ∖ A ) = A ∪ B ∩ A c B=A uu(B\\A)=A uu(B nnA^(c))B=A \cup(B \backslash A)=A \cup\left(B \cap A^{c}\right)B=A∪(B∖A)=A∪(B∩Ac)
Since this union is disjoint, by Postulate 3,
P ( B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ∖ A ) ≥ P ( A ) P ( B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ∖ A ) ≥ P ( A ) P(B)=P(A)+P(B\\A) >= P(A)P(B)=P(A)+P(B \backslash A) \geq P(A)P(B)=P(A)+P(B∖A)≥P(A)
by Postulate 1. Thus P ( A ) ≤ P ( B ) P ( A ) ≤ P ( B ) P(A) <= P(B)P(A) \leq P(B)P(A)≤P(B).
Cor 2.60 ≤ P ( A ) ≤ 1 2.60 ≤ P ( A ) ≤ 1 2.60 <= P(A) <= 12.60 \leq P(A) \leq 12.60≤P(A)≤1 for any event A A AAA.
Pf. ∅ ⊆ A ⊆ S ∅ ⊆ A ⊆ S O/ sube A sube S\varnothing \subseteq A \subseteq S∅⊆A⊆S, so by Thm 2.5,
P ( ∅ ) ≤ P ( A ) ≤ P ( S ) P ( ∅ ) ≤ P ( A ) ≤ P ( S ) P(O/) <= P(A) <= P(S)P(\varnothing) \leq P(A) \leq P(S)P(∅)≤P(A)≤P(S)
That is,
0 ≤ P ( A ) ≤ 1 0 ≤ P ( A ) ≤ 1 0 <= P(A) <= 10 \leq P(A) \leq 10≤P(A)≤1
by Postulate 2 and Cor 2.4.
Thm 2.7 (Addition Rule). If A , B A , B A,BA, BA,B are events in a sample space S S SSS, then
P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) . P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) . P(A uu B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A nn B).P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B) .P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B).
Pf. A ∪ B A ∪ B A uu BA \cup BA∪B can be expressed as a pairwise disjoint union:
A ∪ B = ( A ∖ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( B ∖ A ) . A ∪ B = ( A ∖ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( B ∖ A ) . A uu B=(A\\B)uu(A nn B)uu(B\\A).A \cup B=(A \backslash B) \cup(A \cap B) \cup(B \backslash A) .A∪B=(A∖B)∪(A∩B)∪(B∖A).
So by Postulate 3,
P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ∖ B ) + P ( A ∩ B ) + P ( B ∖ A ) . P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ∖ B ) + P ( A ∩ B ) + P ( B ∖ A ) . P(A uu B)=P(A\\B)+P(A nn B)+P(B\\A).P(A \cup B)=P(A \backslash B)+P(A \cap B)+P(B \backslash A) .P(A∪B)=P(A∖B)+P(A∩B)+P(B∖A).
Also,
P ( A ) = P ( A ∖ B ) + P ( A ∩ B ) , P ( B ) = P ( B ∖ A ) + P ( A ∩ B ) , P ( A ) = P ( A ∖ B ) + P ( A ∩ B ) , P ( B ) = P ( B ∖ A ) + P ( A ∩ B ) , P(A)=P(A\\B)+P(A nn B),quad P(B)=P(B\\A)+P(A nn B),P(A)=P(A \backslash B)+P(A \cap B), \quad P(B)=P(B \backslash A)+P(A \cap B),P(A)=P(A∖B)+P(A∩B),P(B)=P(B∖A)+P(A∩B),
since these are disjoint unions. Substituting gives
P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) . P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) . P(A uu B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A nn B).P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B) .P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B).
Ex. In Chicago, the probabilities are 0.86 , 0.35 , 0.29 0.86 , 0.35 , 0.29 0.86,0.35,0.290.86,0.35,0.290.86,0.35,0.29 that a randomly chosen family owns a colour TV, an HDTV, or both (respectively). What is the probability that a family owns either or both kinds of TV set?
Soln. Let
A = { family owns a colour TV } , B = { family owns a HDTV } . A = {  family owns a colour TV  } , B = {  family owns a HDTV  } . A={" family owns a colour TV "},quad B={" family owns a HDTV "}.A=\{\text { family owns a colour TV }\}, \quad B=\{\text { family owns a HDTV }\} .A={ family owns a colour TV },B={ family owns a HDTV }.
Then A ∩ B = { A ∩ B = { A nn B={A \cap B=\{A∩B={ family owns both types of TV } } }\}}, and we want P ( A ∪ B ) P ( A ∪ B ) P(A uu B)P(A \cup B)P(A∪B). By Thm 2.7,
P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) = 0.86 + 0.35 − 0.29 = 0.92 . P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) = 0.86 + 0.35 − 0.29 = 0.92 .  P(A uu B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A nn B)=0.86+0.35-0.29=0.92". "P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B)=0.86+0.35-0.29=0.92 \text {. }P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B)=0.86+0.35−0.29=0.92. 
Ex. On I-95, Exit 2A, the probability that a truck stopped by the police has faulty brakes is 0.23 and that it has badly worn tyres is 0.24 , while the probability that the truck has faulty brakes and/or worn tyres is 0.38 . What is the probability that a truck has both faulty brakes and worn tyres?
Sup. Let
F = { truck has faulty brakes } , T = { truck has worn tyres } . F = {  truck has faulty brakes  } , T = {  truck has worn tyres  } . F={" truck has faulty brakes "},quad T={" truck has worn tyres "}.F=\{\text { truck has faulty brakes }\}, \quad T=\{\text { truck has worn tyres }\} .F={ truck has faulty brakes },T={ truck has worn tyres }.
We know
P ( F ) = 0.23 , P ( T ) = 0.24 , P ( F ∪ T ) = 0.38 P ( F ) = 0.23 , P ( T ) = 0.24 , P ( F ∪ T ) = 0.38 P(F)=0.23,quad P(T)=0.24,quad P(F uu T)=0.38P(F)=0.23, \quad P(T)=0.24, \quad P(F \cup T)=0.38P(F)=0.23,P(T)=0.24,P(F∪T)=0.38
We want P ( F ∩ T ) P ( F ∩ T ) P(F nn T)P(F \cap T)P(F∩T). By Thm 2.7,
P ( F ∪ T ) = P ( F ) + P ( T ) − P ( F ∩ T ) P ( F ∪ T ) = P ( F ) + P ( T ) − P ( F ∩ T ) P(F uu T)=P(F)+P(T)-P(F nn T)P(F \cup T)=P(F)+P(T)-P(F \cap T)P(F∪T)=P(F)+P(T)−P(F∩T)
So
0.38 = 0.23 + 0.24 − P ( F ∩ T ) 0.38 = 0.23 + 0.24 − P ( F ∩ T ) 0.38=0.23+0.24-P(F nn T)0.38=0.23+0.24-P(F \cap T)0.38=0.23+0.24−P(F∩T)
hence
P ( F ∩ T ) = 0.23 + 0.24 − 0.38 = 0.09 P ( F ∩ T ) = 0.23 + 0.24 − 0.38 = 0.09 P(F nn T)=0.23+0.24-0.38=0.09P(F \cap T)=0.23+0.24-0.38=0.09P(F∩T)=0.23+0.24−0.38=0.09
We can generalize Thm 2.7 to three or more events.
Thm 2.8 If A , B , C A , B , C A,B,CA, B, CA,B,C are events in a sample space S S SSS, then
P ( A ∪ B ∪ C ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) + P ( C ) − P ( A ∩ B ) − P ( A ∩ C ) − P ( B ∩ C ) + P ( A ∩ B ∩ C ) . P ( A ∪ B ∪ C ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) + P ( C ) − P ( A ∩ B ) − P ( A ∩ C ) − P ( B ∩ C ) + P ( A ∩ B ∩ C ) . P(A uu B uu C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A nn B)-P(A nn C)-P(B nn C)+P(A nn B nn C).P(A \cup B \cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A \cap B)-P(A \cap C)-P(B \cap C)+P(A \cap B \cap C) .P(A∪B∪C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)−P(A∩B)−P(A∩C)−P(B∩C)+P(A∩B∩C).
First we prove a lemma (which is an exercise in the book).

Lemma.

A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) = ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) = ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A nn(B uu C)=(A nn B)uu(A nn C)A \cap(B \cup C)=(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)A∩(B∪C)=(A∩B)∪(A∩C)
Pf. A common way of showing two sets (events) E , F E , F E,FE, FE,F are the same set is to first show E ⊆ F E ⊆ F E sube FE \subseteq FE⊆F and then F ⊆ E F ⊆ E F sube EF \subseteq EF⊆E, which then implies that E = F E = F E=FE=FE=F.
Suppose x ∈ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) x ∈ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) x in A nn(B uu C)x \in A \cap(B \cup C)x∈A∩(B∪C). Then x ∈ A x ∈ A x in Ax \in Ax∈A and x ∈ B ∪ C x ∈ B ∪ C x in B uu Cx \in B \cup Cx∈B∪C. Since x ∈ B ∪ C x ∈ B ∪ C x in B uu Cx \in B \cup Cx∈B∪C, we have x ∈ B x ∈ B x in Bx \in Bx∈B or x ∈ C x ∈ C x in Cx \in Cx∈C (or both).
If x ∈ B x ∈ B x in Bx \in Bx∈B, then x ∈ A x ∈ A x in Ax \in Ax∈A also, so x ∈ A ∩ B ⊆ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) x ∈ A ∩ B ⊆ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) x in A nn B sube(A nn B)uu(A nn C)x \in A \cap B \subseteq(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)x∈A∩B⊆(A∩B)∪(A∩C). Similarly, if x ∈ C x ∈ C x in Cx \in Cx∈C, then x ∈ A x ∈ A x in Ax \in Ax∈A also, so x ∈ A ∩ C ⊆ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) x ∈ A ∩ C ⊆ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) x in A nn C sube(A nn B)uu(A nn C)x \in A \cap C \subseteq(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)x∈A∩C⊆(A∩B)∪(A∩C). In either case,
A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) ⊆ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) ⊆ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A nn(B uu C)sube(A nn B)uu(A nn C)A \cap(B \cup C) \subseteq(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)A∩(B∪C)⊆(A∩B)∪(A∩C)
Conversely, suppose x ∈ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) x ∈ ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) x in(A nn B)uu(A nn C)x \in(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)x∈(A∩B)∪(A∩C). Then either x ∈ A ∩ B x ∈ A ∩ B x in A nn Bx \in A \cap Bx∈A∩B or x ∈ A ∩ C x ∈ A ∩ C x in A nn Cx \in A \cap Cx∈A∩C (or both). If x ∈ A ∩ B x ∈ A ∩ B x in A nn Bx \in A \cap Bx∈A∩B, then x ∈ A x ∈ A x in Ax \in Ax∈A and x ∈ B x ∈ B x in Bx \in Bx∈B, so x ∈ B ∪ C x ∈ B ∪ C x in B uu Cx \in B \cup Cx∈B∪C also. Hence x ∈ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) x ∈ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) x in A nn(B uu C)x \in A \cap(B \cup C)x∈A∩(B∪C). Similarly, if x ∈ A ∩ C x ∈ A ∩ C x in A nn Cx \in A \cap Cx∈A∩C, then x ∈ A x ∈ A x in Ax \in Ax∈A and x ∈ C x ∈ C x in Cx \in Cx∈C, so x ∈ B ∪ C x ∈ B ∪ C x in B uu Cx \in B \cup Cx∈B∪C also. Hence x ∈ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) x ∈ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) x in A nn(B uu C)x \in A \cap(B \cup C)x∈A∩(B∪C). Therefore,
( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) ⊆ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) ⊆ A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) (A nn B)uu(A nn C)sube A nn(B uu C)(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C) \subseteq A \cap(B \cup C)(A∩B)∪(A∩C)⊆A∩(B∪C)
Thus
A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) = ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) = ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A nn(B uu C)=(A nn B)uu(A nn C)A \cap(B \cup C)=(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)A∩(B∪C)=(A∩B)∪(A∩C)
as required.
Now back to business -
Pf. of Thm 2.8. Write A ∪ B ∪ C A ∪ B ∪ C A uu B uu CA \cup B \cup CA∪B∪C as A ∪ ( B ∪ C ) A ∪ ( B ∪ C ) A uu(B uu C)A \cup(B \cup C)A∪(B∪C) and apply Thm 2.7:
P ( A ∪ B ∪ C ) = P ( A ∪ ( B ∪ C ) ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ∪ C ) − P ( A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) ) . P ( A ∪ B ∪ C ) = P ( A ∪ ( B ∪ C ) ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ∪ C ) − P ( A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) ) . P(A uu B uu C)=P(A uu(B uu C))=P(A)+P(B uu C)-P(A nn(B uu C)).P(A \cup B \cup C)=P(A \cup(B \cup C))=P(A)+P(B \cup C)-P(A \cap(B \cup C)) .P(A∪B∪C)=P(A∪(B∪C))=P(A)+P(B∪C)−P(A∩(B∪C)).
By Thm 2.7 on B B BBB and C C CCC,
P ( B ∪ C ) = P ( B ) + P ( C ) − P ( B ∩ C ) P ( B ∪ C ) = P ( B ) + P ( C ) − P ( B ∩ C ) P(B uu C)=P(B)+P(C)-P(B nn C)P(B \cup C)=P(B)+P(C)-P(B \cap C)P(B∪C)=P(B)+P(C)−P(B∩C)
By the lemma,
A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) = ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A ∩ ( B ∪ C ) = ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) A nn(B uu C)=(A nn B)uu(A nn C)A \cap(B \cup C)=(A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C)A∩(B∪C)=(A∩B)∪(A∩C)
Applying Thm 2.7 again,
P ( ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) ) = P ( A ∩ B ) + P ( A ∩ C ) − P ( ( A ∩ B ) ∩ ( A ∩ C ) ) . P ( ( A ∩ B ) ∪ ( A ∩ C ) ) = P ( A ∩ B ) + P ( A ∩ C ) − P ( ( A ∩ B ) ∩ ( A ∩ C ) ) . P((A nn B)uu(A nn C))=P(A nn B)+P(A nn C)-P((A nn B)nn(A nn C)).P((A \cap B) \cup(A \cap C))=P(A \cap B)+P(A \cap C)-P((A \cap B) \cap(A \cap C)) .P((A∩B)∪(A∩C))=P(A∩B)+P(A∩C)−P((A∩B)∩(A∩C)).
Since
( A ∩ B ) ∩ ( A ∩ C ) = A ∩ B ∩ C ( A ∩ B ) ∩ ( A ∩ C ) = A ∩ B ∩ C (A nn B)nn(A nn C)=A nn B nn C(A \cap B) \cap(A \cap C)=A \cap B \cap C(A∩B)∩(A∩C)=A∩B∩C
we obtain
P ( A ∪ B ∪ C ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) + P ( C ) − P ( A ∩ B ) − P ( A ∩ C ) − P ( B ∩ C ) + P ( A ∩ B ∩ C ) . P ( A ∪ B ∪ C ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) + P ( C ) − P ( A ∩ B ) − P ( A ∩ C ) − P ( B ∩ C ) + P ( A ∩ B ∩ C ) . P(A uu B uu C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A nn B)-P(A nn C)-P(B nn C)+P(A nn B nn C).P(A \cup B \cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A \cap B)-P(A \cap C)-P(B \cap C)+P(A \cap B \cap C) .P(A∪B∪C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)−P(A∩B)−P(A∩C)−P(B∩C)+P(A∩B∩C).
Ex. Girls at Redean play hockey, netball, and lacrosse. We are given the following information that for a randomly chosen girl,
P ( plays hockey ) = 0.6 , P ( plays netball ) = 0.5 , P ( plays lacrosse ) = 0.45 , P ( plays hockey and netball ) = 0.25 , P ( plays hockey and lacrosse ) = 0.2 , P ( plays netball and lacrosse ) = 0.25 , P ( plays at least one sport ) = 0.9 . P (  plays hockey  ) = 0.6 , P (  plays netball  ) = 0.5 , P (  plays lacrosse  ) = 0.45 , P (  plays hockey and netball  ) = 0.25 , P (  plays hockey and lacrosse  ) = 0.2 , P (  plays netball and lacrosse  ) = 0.25 , P (  plays at least one sport  ) = 0.9 . {:[P(" plays hockey ")=0.6","],[P(" plays netball ")=0.5","],[P(" plays lacrosse ")=0.45","],[P(" plays hockey and netball ")=0.25","],[P(" plays hockey and lacrosse ")=0.2","],[P(" plays netball and lacrosse ")=0.25","],[P(" plays at least one sport ")=0.9.]:}\begin{gathered} P(\text { plays hockey })=0.6, \\ P(\text { plays netball })=0.5, \\ P(\text { plays lacrosse })=0.45, \\ P(\text { plays hockey and netball })=0.25, \\ P(\text { plays hockey and lacrosse })=0.2, \\ P(\text { plays netball and lacrosse })=0.25, \\ P(\text { plays at least one sport })=0.9 . \end{gathered}P( plays hockey )=0.6,P( plays netball )=0.5,P( plays lacrosse )=0.45,P( plays hockey and netball )=0.25,P( plays hockey and lacrosse )=0.2,P( plays netball and lacrosse )=0.25,P( plays at least one sport )=0.9.
What is the probability a randomly chosen girl plays
  1. all three sports,
  2. hockey only,
  3. netball and hockey only?
Soln. Let H , N , L H , N , L H,N,LH, N, LH,N,L stand for the events that a girl plays hockey, netball, and lacrosse respectively. We are given
P ( H ) = 0.6 , P ( N ) = 0.5 , P ( L ) = 0.45 , P ( H ∩ N ) = 0.25 , P ( H ∩ L ) = 0.2 , P ( N ∩ L ) = 0.25 , P ( H ) = 0.6 , P ( N ) = 0.5 , P ( L ) = 0.45 , P ( H ∩ N ) = 0.25 , P ( H ∩ L ) = 0.2 , P ( N ∩ L ) = 0.25 , {:[P(H)=0.6","quad P(N)=0.5","quad P(L)=0.45","],[P(H nn N)=0.25","quad P(H nn L)=0.2","quad P(N nn L)=0.25","]:}\begin{gathered} P(H)=0.6, \quad P(N)=0.5, \quad P(L)=0.45, \\ P(H \cap N)=0.25, \quad P(H \cap L)=0.2, \quad P(N \cap L)=0.25, \end{gathered}P(H)=0.6,P(N)=0.5,P(L)=0.45,P(H∩N)=0.25,P(H∩L)=0.2,P(N∩L)=0.25,
and
P ( H ∪ N ∪ L ) = 0.9 . P ( H ∪ N ∪ L ) = 0.9 .  P(H uu N uu L)=0.9". "P(H \cup N \cup L)=0.9 \text {. }P(H∪N∪L)=0.9. 
(a) By Thm 2.8,
P ( H ∪ N ∪ L ) = P ( H ) + P ( N ) + P ( L ) − P ( H ∩ N ) − P ( H ∩ L ) − P ( N ∩ L ) + P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) . P ( H ∪ N ∪ L ) = P ( H ) + P ( N ) + P ( L ) − P ( H ∩ N ) − P ( H ∩ L ) − P ( N ∩ L ) + P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) . P(H uu N uu L)=P(H)+P(N)+P(L)-P(H nn N)-P(H nn L)-P(N nn L)+P(H nn N nn L).P(H \cup N \cup L)=P(H)+P(N)+P(L)-P(H \cap N)-P(H \cap L)-P(N \cap L)+P(H \cap N \cap L) .P(H∪N∪L)=P(H)+P(N)+P(L)−P(H∩N)−P(H∩L)−P(N∩L)+P(H∩N∩L).
So
0.9 = 0.6 + 0.5 + 0.45 − 0.25 − 0.2 − 0.25 + P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) 0.9 = 0.6 + 0.5 + 0.45 − 0.25 − 0.2 − 0.25 + P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) 0.9=0.6+0.5+0.45-0.25-0.2-0.25+P(H nn N nn L)0.9=0.6+0.5+0.45-0.25-0.2-0.25+P(H \cap N \cap L)0.9=0.6+0.5+0.45−0.25−0.2−0.25+P(H∩N∩L)
hence
P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) = 0.05 P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) = 0.05 P(H nn N nn L)=0.05P(H \cap N \cap L)=0.05P(H∩N∩L)=0.05
(b) Hockey only is represented by the event H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) H\\(N uu L)H \backslash(N \cup L)H∖(N∪L).
Now
H = ( H ∩ ( N ∪ L ) ) ∪ ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) , H = ( H ∩ ( N ∪ L ) ) ∪ ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) , H=(H nn(N uu L))uu(H\\(N uu L)),H=(H \cap(N \cup L)) \cup(H \backslash(N \cup L)),H=(H∩(N∪L))∪(H∖(N∪L)),
and this union is disjoint, so by Postulate 3,
P ( H ) = P ( H ∩ ( N ∪ L ) ) + P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) . P ( H ) = P ( H ∩ ( N ∪ L ) ) + P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) . P(H)=P(H nn(N uu L))+P(H\\(N uu L)).P(H)=P(H \cap(N \cup L))+P(H \backslash(N \cup L)) .P(H)=P(H∩(N∪L))+P(H∖(N∪L)).
By the lemma,
H ∩ ( N ∪ L ) = ( H ∩ N ) ∪ ( H ∩ L ) H ∩ ( N ∪ L ) = ( H ∩ N ) ∪ ( H ∩ L ) H nn(N uu L)=(H nn N)uu(H nn L)H \cap(N \cup L)=(H \cap N) \cup(H \cap L)H∩(N∪L)=(H∩N)∪(H∩L)
Therefore, by Thm 2.7,
P ( H ) = P ( H ∩ N ) + P ( H ∩ L ) − P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∩ ( H ∩ L ) ) + P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) . P ( H ) = P ( H ∩ N ) + P ( H ∩ L ) − P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∩ ( H ∩ L ) ) + P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) . P(H)=P(H nn N)+P(H nn L)-P((H nn N)nn(H nn L))+P(H\\(N uu L)).P(H)=P(H \cap N)+P(H \cap L)-P((H \cap N) \cap(H \cap L))+P(H \backslash(N \cup L)) .P(H)=P(H∩N)+P(H∩L)−P((H∩N)∩(H∩L))+P(H∖(N∪L)).
Since
( H ∩ N ) ∩ ( H ∩ L ) = H ∩ N ∩ L , ( H ∩ N ) ∩ ( H ∩ L ) = H ∩ N ∩ L , (H nn N)nn(H nn L)=H nn N nn L,(H \cap N) \cap(H \cap L)=H \cap N \cap L,(H∩N)∩(H∩L)=H∩N∩L,
we get
0.6 = 0.25 + 0.2 − 0.05 + P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) 0.6 = 0.25 + 0.2 − 0.05 + P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) 0.6=0.25+0.2-0.05+P(H\\(N uu L))0.6=0.25+0.2-0.05+P(H \backslash(N \cup L))0.6=0.25+0.2−0.05+P(H∖(N∪L))
So
P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) = 0.2 P ( H ∖ ( N ∪ L ) ) = 0.2 P(H\\(N uu L))=0.2P(H \backslash(N \cup L))=0.2P(H∖(N∪L))=0.2
(c) Hockey and netball only is represented by the event ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L (H nn N)\\L(H \cap N) \backslash L(H∩N)∖L.
Now
H ∩ N = ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) ∪ ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) H ∩ N = ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) ∪ ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) H nn N=(H nn N nn L)uu((H nn N)\\L)H \cap N=(H \cap N \cap L) \cup((H \cap N) \backslash L)H∩N=(H∩N∩L)∪((H∩N)∖L)
and this union is disjoint, so by Postulate 3,
P ( H ∩ N ) = P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) + P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) . P ( H ∩ N ) = P ( H ∩ N ∩ L ) + P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) . P(H nn N)=P(H nn N nn L)+P((H nn N)\\L).P(H \cap N)=P(H \cap N \cap L)+P((H \cap N) \backslash L) .P(H∩N)=P(H∩N∩L)+P((H∩N)∖L).
Thus
0.25 = 0.05 + P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) 0.25 = 0.05 + P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) 0.25=0.05+P((H nn N)\\L)0.25=0.05+P((H \cap N) \backslash L)0.25=0.05+P((H∩N)∖L)
so
P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) = 0.2 P ( ( H ∩ N ) ∖ L ) = 0.2 P((H nn N)\\L)=0.2P((H \cap N) \backslash L)=0.2P((H∩N)∖L)=0.2
Ex. Fill in the rest of the Venn diagram.
We can obviously intersect any number of events. FYI the formula in the general case is:
Thm. If A 1 , A 2 , … , A n A 1 , A 2 , … , A n A_(1),A_(2),dots,A_(n)A_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{n}A1,A2,…,An are n n nnn events in a sample space S S SSS, then
P ( ⋃ i = 1 n A i ) = ∑ i = 1 n ( ( − 1 ) i − 1 ∑ 1 ≤ j 1 < j 2 < ⋯ < j i ≤ n P ( ⋂ k = 1 i A j k ) ) . P ⋃ i = 1 n   A i = ∑ i = 1 n   ( − 1 ) i − 1 ∑ 1 ≤ j 1 < j 2 < ⋯ < j i ≤ n   P ⋂ k = 1 i   A j k . P(uuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i))=sum_(i=1)^(n)((-1)^(i-1)sum_(1 <= j_(1) < j_(2) < cdots < j_(i) <= n)P(nnn_(k=1)^(i)A_(j_(k)))).P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left((-1)^{i-1} \sum_{1 \leq j_{1}<j_{2}<\cdots<j_{i} \leq n} P\left(\bigcap_{k=1}^{i} A_{j_{k}}\right)\right) .P(⋃i=1nAi)=∑i=1n((−1)i−1∑1≤j1<j2<⋯<ji≤nP(⋂k=1iAjk)).
I do NOT expect you to memorize this!
